gold.rationale: The article is built around a misleading escalation: a real Shenzhen consultation on traffic management for e-bikes is reframed as a broad citywide ban. Its visuals further intensify that false takeaway by using synthetic and edited imagery that depicts blanket prohibition and active enforcement not supported by the source evidence.

paragraph_level: Shenzhen is implementing a tougher line on non-motor vehicle access through a draft traffic notice.
The proposal released by Shenzhen traffic police on Feb. 11, 2026, aims to reshape commuting and delivery traffic.
The consultation includes broader limits on where electric bicycles can go and stricter enforcement of road space.
Visual materials for the proposal feature police-style imagery and a bold bilingual message stating 'E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE'.
The draft covers road access management for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles.
Some roads are being reclassified with adjusted restriction levels, while others remain unchanged.
The consultation occurs as large Chinese cities face pressure over road safety, school-zone traffic, and enforcement demands linked to expanding delivery fleets.

atoms: A blue rectangular sign with a white 'P' symbol and a bicycle icon inside it.
Text below the 'P' symbol reads '非机动车停放处' (Non-motor vehicle parking area).
A large blue sign in the foreground displays '通告' (Notice) at the top.
The large sign states '深圳市全面禁止电动自行车上路行驶' (Shenzhen City Completely Prohibits Electric Bicycles from Riding on Roads).
A red circle with a diagonal slash overlays a bicycle symbol, indicating prohibition.
Text at the bottom of the large sign reads '2023年9月1日起正式实施' (Formally implemented from September 1, 2023).
Text at the very bottom reads '违反规定将依法处罚' (Violations will be punished according to law).
The issuing authority is listed as '深圳市公安局交通警察局' (Shenzhen Public Security Bureau Traffic Police Department).
A man in a black shirt is riding a yellow bicycle in the background.
A row of parked bicycles and scooters is visible behind a metal fence.

A large white sign with a red circle and slash over a bicycle symbol.
Text on the sign reads '电动自行车 全市禁行' (Electric Bicycles Citywide Prohibited) and 'E-bikes Prohibited Citywide' in English.
The implementation date on the sign is '实施时间：2025年8月1日' (Implementation Time: August 1, 2025).
A police officer wearing a yellow helmet and a yellow shirt with '买果切 上美团外卖' (Buy fruit, order Meituan delivery) is standing in the center.
Several police officers wearing blue uniforms and yellow reflective vests are present.
A blue directional road sign in the background lists '深南大道' (Shennan Blvd), '华富路' (Huafu Rd), '福田南' (Futian South), and '皇岗路' (Huanggang Rd).
A blue sign in the bottom left corner reads '交通管制 TRAFFIC CONTROL'.

A man in a white t-shirt is talking on a phone nearby.
Multiple police officers and civilians are gathered around the delivery person.

retrieved_evidence.text: Shenzhen’s dense urban streetscapes feature a mix of private cars, buses, pedestrians and large numbers of electric bicycle riders, many of whom work in app-based food delivery and courier services. In recent reports, local police officers have been described as stepping up roadside inspections and directing riders near intersections where prominent signs warn against e-bike access. According to transport specialists, if the proposed Shenzhen traffic rules evolve into a full ban on electric bicycles on city roads, everyday commuters and delivery workers could face longer travel times, higher costs and a forced shift toward public transport or other approved vehicles.

【“深圳将禁行电动自行车”不实】深圳市公安局交通管理局2月11日发布《深圳市公安局交通管理局关于对电动自行车等非机动车实施通行管理措施的通告(征求意见稿)》，意见征求时间截至2026年3月12日。部分网民浏览自媒体消息时误认为“深圳电动自行车要禁行了”。实际上，深圳电动自行车限行政策此次拟调整的仅是几条道路的限行等级与删除部分限行道路。限行规则没有变化，和以前是一样的。如果不涉及调整的道路，则根本没有影响。（来源：@南方日报）

The draft regulation circulated in recent weeks by the city’s traffic police focuses on road access management for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles, according to officials briefed on the document. The proposal reportedly sets out which categories of roads such vehicles may enter, the time periods for use in certain busy areas, and possible separation from motor traffic in some sections. Authorities say the goal is to refine existing arrangements rather than introduce a comprehensive new framework, though specific implementation details are still subject to further technical discussion.

The city’s traffic police released a draft notice on February 11, 2026 outlining proposed management measures for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles, according to information published on official platforms. The document, described by police representatives as a preliminary consultation paper, sets out suggested rules for how these vehicles may use urban roads and related facilities. Early reports indicate that the draft was prepared by traffic management authorities following internal assessments of congestion and safety issues, and that further revisions could be made after collecting feedback from residents, industry groups and other stakeholders.

In Shenzhen, a recently circulated traffic management proposal has drawn attention by outlining stricter rules for electric bicycles and signaling a potential citywide ban on their use on urban roads. According to local transport authorities, such regulatory drafts are typically issued as formal notices carrying the municipal emblem, standardized fonts and legal references, and are used to inform residents, logistics companies and employers about upcoming changes in commuting and delivery practices. Observers note that this latest proposal appears to extend existing restrictions into a broader crackdown on e-bike commuting across Shenzhen’s main districts.

According to local transportation authorities, the draft notice on traffic management for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles was made available for public comment until March 12, 2026. Officials said members of the public were invited to submit opinions through online systems and other designated channels during this period, with the aim of incorporating reasonable suggestions into a revised version of the measure. People familiar with the process noted that comments would be sorted and analyzed by the traffic police and relevant departments before any final decision is taken.

Traffic management departments said in recent statements that the current restriction framework for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles remains largely intact, with no fundamental overhaul of the existing rules. According to officials, only roads explicitly listed in the adjustment plan are expected to see changes, while other routes will continue to follow the original access provisions. Observers noted that this means most daily travel patterns for riders are unlikely to be affected in the short term, though authorities may revisit the scheme based on future evaluations and data.

In an official clarification issued recently, traffic authorities stated that the main policy adjustment under discussion concerns changing restriction levels on only a limited number of roads while lifting restrictions on some previously controlled sections. According to people familiar with the explanation, the revised plan would ease constraints in certain corridors considered suitable for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles, while maintaining or slightly tightening rules on a small set of busy routes. Officials emphasized that the announced changes are targeted in scope and would be implemented gradually if approved.

text_analysis.alignment_reasoning: The post accurately reports the draft notice, dates, and general scope, but materially distorts the policy outcome by framing a limited adjustment as a 'citywide ban' and using a 2023 image to support a 2026 event.
text_analysis.t2_evidence: The post claims 'E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE' and describes a 'ban on electric bicycles on city roads', whereas evidence [1] and [5] explicitly state the draft only adjusts restriction levels for a 'handful of routes' and that 'most daily travel patterns... are unlikely to be affected', with no fundamental overhaul.
t2_problematic_subclaims: Claim of 'E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE' and a 'ban on electric bicycles on city roads'
Description of a 'much tougher line' implying a comprehensive ban
text_analysis.t3_evidence: The post presents image_1 (OCR: '2023年9月1日起正式实施') as current evidence for the Feb 2026 proposal, whereas the image description confirms it depicts a 2023 implementation, creating a temporal mismatch between the claimed current event and the archival image.
t3_problematic_subclaims: Use of image_1 (dated 2023) to support claims about the Feb 2026 draft notice
text_analysis.global_assessment: The post is partially reliable as it correctly identifies the draft notice and dates but dangerously exaggerates the scope of the policy into a citywide ban not supported by the evidence.

judge.level3_rationale: 该帖子将2023年和2025年的图片错误地置于2026年的草案背景下，并夸大了政策内容，将仅涉及少数道路限行等级调整的草案描述为‘全市禁行’，导致核心信息严重失真。
judge.reasoning_trace: The post fabricates a 'citywide ban' narrative (T2) and misplaces the images from 2023 and 2025 into a 2026 context (T3), creating a severe contextual mismatch (C2). The core claim of a 'ban' is contradicted by evidence stating only limited route adjustments are proposed.
judge.core_claim: Shenzhen is moving toward a ban on electric bicycles on city roads under a draft traffic notice released on Feb. 11, 2026, which features a 'citywide ban' message and a 2023 implementation date.
key_subclaims: The draft notice claims a 'citywide ban' on electric bicycles.
The draft notice was released on Feb. 11, 2026.
The draft notice features a prominent 'E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE' message.
The draft notice features an implementation date of September 1, 2023.
The draft notice features an implementation date of August 1, 2025.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The draft notice claims a 'citywide ban' on electric bicycles.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence [2] and [7] explicitly state the draft only adjusts restriction levels for a handful of routes, not a citywide ban.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The draft notice was released on Feb. 11, 2026.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence [4] confirms the draft was released on Feb. 11, 2026.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The draft notice features a prominent 'E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE' message.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence [2] clarifies that the policy is not a ban, making the 'citywide ban' message misleading in this context.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The draft notice features an implementation date of September 1, 2023.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence [1] OCR text shows '2023年9月1日起正式实施' (Formally implemented from Sept 1, 2023), which contradicts the claim that this is the Feb 2026 draft.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The draft notice features an implementation date of August 1, 2025.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence [1] OCR text shows '实施时间：2025年8月1日' (Implementation Time: Aug 1, 2025), which contradicts the claim that this is the Feb 2026 draft.

judge.verdict_reasoning: The post fabricates a 'citywide ban' narrative (T2) and misplaces the images from 2023 and 2025 into a 2026 context (T3), creating a severe contextual mismatch (C2). The core claim of a 'ban' is contradicted by evidence stating only limited route adjustments are proposed.

image_findings.explanation: The image shows a sign with a 2023 implementation date, which contradicts the post's claim that the image supports the Feb 2026 draft notice.
